items.q: Is illegally copying music an offence under the Computer Misuse Act?
items.correct: No - unauthorised copying of creative works falls under the Copyright, Designs and Patents Act 1988; the Computer Misuse Act covers unauthorised access and damage to systems.
items.misconception: Yes - the Computer Misuse Act covers any illegal activity involving a computer.
items.random_wrong: Yes - music piracy is covered by the Data Protection Act.
items.why: Because a computer is involved, students reach for the CMA. Match the offence to the interest protected: access/integrity (CMA), creative works (CDPA), personal data (DPA).

items.q: Does personal data only mean secret or sensitive information?
items.correct: No - personal data is any information relating to an identified or identifiable living person, including names, email addresses and online identifiers.
items.misconception: Yes - only secrets like medical records count as personal data.
items.random_wrong: Yes - data stops being personal once it is posted online.
items.why: Students set the bar too high. The legal definition is broad - identifiability, not secrecy, is the test - which is why even IP addresses can be personal data.

items.q: Is the digital divide only about who owns devices?
items.correct: No - it also covers differences in connectivity quality, digital skills and confidence, so someone with a device may still be digitally excluded.
items.misconception: Yes - once everyone has a smartphone the divide is closed.
items.random_wrong: Yes - the digital divide refers to splitting files between two disks.
items.why: Hardware is the visible part. Meaningful access requires affordable connectivity and the skills to use services, which is why the divide persists in wealthy countries.

items.q: The Regulation of Investigatory Powers Act 2000 primarily regulates:
items.correct: Surveillance and interception of communications by public bodies
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Surveillance and interception of communications by public bodies. “The price of broadband contracts” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: The Copyright, Designs and Patents Act 1988 protects:
items.correct: Original works, including software, from unauthorised copying
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Original works, including software, from unauthorised copying. “Computers from physical theft” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Creating and distributing malware to impair computers is an offence under:
items.why: Correct answer: The Computer Misuse Act 1990. “The Data Protection Act 2018” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.correct: The gap between those with effective access to digital technology and skills and those without
items.misconception: The split between analogue and digital broadcasting
items.random_wrong: Dividing a hard disk into partitions
items.why: The answer examiners want is: The gap between those with effective access to digital technology and skills and those without. “The split between analogue and digital broadcasting” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In what year was the Copyright, Designs and Patents Act passed in the UK?
items.why: The real figure is 1988. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
